pages.text: SUSTAINABILITY REPORT 2024 Sustainability. Driven. RACING. GREEN.

Our journey building a world-leading sustainable ultra-luxury automotive business continues. It is a key focus of our corporate strategy and the central objective of our sustainability strategy, Racing. Green. The report is produced by the Aston Martin Sustainability team under the supervision of the Board Sustainability Committee. We publish a sustainability report annually to disclose information to all stakeholders. The report follows international guidelines and best practices including reporting in line with the principles of the Global Reporting Initiative (‘GRI’) Standards (GRI: Foundation 2021). Our disclosure related to climate change is based on the recommendations of the Task Force on Climate-Related Financial Disclosures. We also consider and have regard to the UK’s Mandatory Climate Disclosure requirements. The report is published as a PDF on our website and a small number of reports are available in hard-copy on request. If you have questions about this report, please contact us by email: sustainability@astonmartin.com Reporting period: The Report covers the period 1 January 2024 to 31 December 2024. Regarding Scope 3 emissions, this report utilises 2023 data because of the significant data requirements and time needed to secure this data from the wider value chain. Scope and boundaries: The Report covers the activities of Aston Martin Lagonda Group Holdings plc and its subsidiaries (referred to as Aston Martin for convenience in this report) – all of which are outlined in the Aston Martin Lagonda Group Holdings Plc Annual Report, available on our website at www.astonmartin.com./corporate Independent assurance: Selected performance data in this Report is subject to limited assurance. The independent assurance report is included within this Report. This assurance on selected information and key performance indicators is in accordance with the International Standard on Assurance Engagements (‘ISAE’) 3000. The statement sets out the data that is assured in this Report. Aston Martin publishes several reporting documents, including for reasons of regulatory compliance, which aim to meet the interests and reporting requirements of a range of stakeholders. pages.text: Introduction from our Chief Executive Officer Adrian Hallmark joined Aston Martin as Chief Executive Officer in September 2024 with almost 30 years experience in the luxury automotive sector including senior leadership roles at Bentley Motors, Porsche GB and Jaguar Land Rover. He reflects on the role of sustainability in Aston Martin’s business and the progress of Aston Martin’s ‘Racing. Green.’ sustainability strategy. WHAT ARE YOUR FIRST IMPRESSIONS OF ASTON MARTIN’S APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY? The business has come a long way in a short period of time since the launch of the Racing. Green. sustainability strategy in 2022. Foundational actions such as data capture and analysis have been completed to provide a clear start point for the definition of effective action. As a primary motivator, we will use our sustainability strategy to be a value driver for the Company, enabling us to create a step change in the efficiency of our operations. This internal benefit creates a win-win, as it is equally aligned with the needs of our customers, supply chain partners, employees and investors. I have witnessed huge enthusiasm across the business to make sure we have a deep understanding of our environmental and social impacts so we can build credible, robust and implementable plans to deliver our sustainability objectives. Significant effort has been invested in making sustainability part of how the Company works, including both its processes and culture. I have appreciated the strong focus on people, keeping our people safe and well is central to everything we do and I’m proud that this year we continued to improve our safety performance. This is a topic which requires relentless effort, and we will always keep safety as our top priority. It’s also been pleasing to see the breadth of sustainability-related training taking place, from modern slavery to our Code of Conduct and carbon literacy, as well as how many employees are volunteering their time to engage and support the local communities that we are part of. pages.text: “INNOVATION IS THE ENGINE OF OUR JOURNEY.” HOW IMPORTANT IS SUSTAINABILITY FOR A COMPANY LIKE ASTON MARTIN? Sustainability is fundamental to Aston Martin’s mission of delivering ultra-luxury high performance and simultaneously meeting the evolving expectations of a changing world. The global business environment, heavily shaped by challenges such as climate change is leading to regulatory shifts and consumer demands for ever improving ethical business practices, requires us to make progress. By committing to sustainability, we aim to make a positive contribution to society. The world expects businesses to take a responsible approach, managing impacts and adding value through the creation of jobs, economic growth, investing in innovation and supporting local communities. ASTON MARTIN HAS UPDATED ITS SUSTAINABILITY STRATEGY. WHY? Now that we have understood our profile and areas for significant innovation, we have chosen to also create more focus on the priority areas of action and align to simplified and stretch goals. This will pave the way for the launch of Aston Martin’s first BEV, planned for the latter part of this decade. This phased approach reflects the Company’s strategy to offer a diverse range of powertrain options, including electrified vehicles that will leverage our strategic partnerships and cutting-edge high-performance technologies, ensuring an unparalleled driving experience for customers. The successful transition to electrification is heavily dependent upon factors not in our control such as the establishment of comprehensive and low-cost charging infrastructure. Action to deliver the relevant infrastructure will affect the scale and pace at which the industry can move. These wider transition measures will substantially affect consumer demand for PHEVs and BEVs. WHERE DO YOU SEE OPPORTUNITIES FOR INNOVATION TO DRIVE SUSTAINABILITY? A focus on sustainability can drive innovation and thereby create value and a competitive advantage. Innovation is the engine of our sustainability journey. There are significant opportunities in areas like lightweight materials, circular manufacturing processes and electric powertrains. For instance, through Project ELEVATION, the development of modular An example of this focus is how we have strengthened our climate change-related ambitions by setting new targets to reduce our direct and indirect greenhouse gas (‘GHG’) emissions by 42% by 2030 and by 90% by 2050 (from a 2022 baseline). This level of effort, which will be challenging, aligns with the rate of reduction which global climate experts such as the Science Based Targets initiative say is needed from the automotive sector to address the challenge of global warming. Alongside a revision of our Racing. Green. targets, we have taken steps to improve our sustainability processes. This includes an updated Responsible Procurement Policy setting out the minimum standards we expect from all our suppliers. We are also integrating sustainability into both our operational and product development processes. WHAT IS ASTON MARTIN’S PLAN FOR ELECTRIFICATION GIVEN THIS IS A KEY AREA TO ADDRESS CLIMATE CHANGE? We recognise that transitioning from internal combustion engines (‘ICE’) to electrification is a key priority for addressing GHG emissions from the automotive sector. Simultaneously, we are developing alternatives to the ICE, with a blended drivetrain approach to our portfolio between 2025 and 2030, including Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (‘PHEV’) and Battery Electric Vehicles (‘BEV’), with a clear plan to have a line-up of electrified sports cars and SUVs. In response to customer feedback and evolving market dynamics, Aston Martin is prioritising the adoption of PHEV technology, beginning with Valhalla, before transferring the knowledge and technology across to our core model range. pages.text: BEV platforms, centred on recyclability and supported by our partnerships with technology leaders, can be a game-changer. The use of recycled materials and advancements like 3D printing, not only reduce waste but also set new benchmarks for performance and design. I’m particularly pleased Aston Martin is actively collaborating with others to take forward sustainability related innovation through projects working with leading universities, as well as established industry leaders and world-class start-ups with breakthrough ideas. Project PIVOT (page 28) is a great example of this collaborative approach where we are working with partners to explore how to significantly reduce CO2 emissions from aluminium, which accounts for the largest emissions impact from a material in our vehicles. WHAT DO YOU SEE AS ASTON MARTIN’S PRIORITIES FOR SUSTAINABILITY IN 2025? In 2024, we improved biodiversity at our manufacturing sites, improved our safety performance and reduced the amount of water in our manufacturing operations. The lower number of cars built compared to our 2024 goal meant that our CO2e and energy intensity per car increased slightly from 2023, but we expect to get back on track through efficiency improvements identified in our Net Zero plan. The volume and depth of data we gather continues to improve, giving us a greater ability to focus efforts in areas such as waste and energy minimisation and new delivery structures including greater engagement from senior leaders across the Company will help drive this progress. “I’M PARTICULARLY PLEASED ASTON MARTIN IS ACTIVELY COLLABORATING WITH OTHERS TO TAKE FORWARD SUSTAINABILITY RELATED INNOVATION THROUGH PROJECTS WORKING WITH LEADING UNIVERSITIES, AS WELL AS ESTABLISHED INDUSTRY LEADERS AND WORLD-CLASS START-UPS WITH BREAKTHROUGH IDEAS.” EMPLOYEE TOWN HALL, DECEMBER 2024 The coming months and years ahead must be about action, not just in our own operations but across our whole value chain. We will continue to work with suppliers and other partners to make progress. Racing. Green. sets the direction for strong sustainability performance to drive efficiency and innovation. Our commercial and financial goals, in combination with processes that integrate sustainability across all aspects of the business from procurement to manufacturing, will deliver long-term value creation and sustainable growth. There is more to do but we are accelerating and moving in the right direction. pages.text: OUR BUSINESS STRATEGY About Aston Martin Aston Martin is an iconic, globally recognised automotive manufacturer. For more than a century, our brand has symbolised exclusivity, elegance, power, beauty, sophistication, innovation, performance and an exceptional standard of styling and design. Our manufacturing sites and headquarters are in the United Kingdom (‘UK’), employing 3,006 people in total. All our cars are hand-built in the UK. Around 6% of colleagues are employed overseas, primarily in commercial and sales roles. To become a world-leading sustainable ultra-luxury automotive business, we are working to embed sustainability across every part of our business. OUR PROMISE Our business strategy is built on the key strengths of brand, product innovation and our people, the pillars that drive our strategy and future growth ambitions. Our fourth strategic pillar and ‘Our promise’ is our sustainability strategy, Racing. Green. which cuts across each of our other strategic pillars. Supporting our business model to create vehicles with the ultimate technology, precision and craftsmanship. TO ENSURE SUCCESS THAT: Elevates sustainability in our brand through collaborations with suppliers, our dealerships and partners delivering innovative solutions and integrating sustainability along our value chain. Builds trust in us by delivering the highest standards of governance and integrity which define everything we do. Focuses on integrating sustainable innovation in new materials, incorporating cutting-edge technology, maximising resource efficiency and building a circular economy. Delivers our net zero ambition as we transition to ICE alternatives with a blended drivetrain approach, integrating whole life cycle thinking built on a transparent and traceable supply chain. Strengthens workforce skills, knowledge and capability through ongoing investment in our people and training, supporting a just transition as we move towards ICE alternatives. Embeds a health and safety mindset as a core priority, cultivating a culture of safety and wellbeing. The global context Aston Martin’s approach to sustainability reflects a complex and fast changing world. Even as we seek to grow our business, we are operating in an external environment shaped by a wide range of economic, geopolitical, societal and environmental challenges. CLIMATE CHANGE Climate change remains the leading global sustainability issue. The United Nations (‘UN’) has reported that the world’s GHG emissions rose to a new high in 2023. Alongside this, average global temperatures have increased significantly, with climate scientists and meteorologists reporting associated warming effects contributing to extreme weather events, community impacts and supply chain disruption. While the UN report it remains technically possible to keep on track to limit global warming to 1.5oC relative to pre-industrial levels, achieving this will require action by all parts of society, including consumers, governments and industry with action in all areas, on a scale and at a pace never seen before. Strategy frameworks and regulations such as the European Union’s (‘EU’) Green Deal and the UK Government’s Net Zero strategy are pushing companies to reduce carbon emissions through policy and regulatory measures like the EU’s Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism and the UK’s Vehicle Emissions Trading Scheme. CLIMATE POLICY While many governments agree on the need for action to address climate change, there is uncertainty around climate policies, including those associated with the automotive sector. For example, the new UK Government elected in July 2024, opened a consultation in December on bringing forward a ban on the sale of new ICE vehicles from 2035 to 2030. The European Automobile Manufacturers’ Association has urged the EU to reconsider deadlines for CO2 emissions targets and an ICE ban by 2035, given falling demand for electric vehicles (‘EV’) in Europe. The most recent figures from the UK’s automotive industry association, the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (‘SMMT’) show that private buyers interest in EVs remains relatively low with these cars making up around one in ten of all cars sold in the UK, a level far below the rate set by the Government. This volatility, uncertainty and policy divergence on approaches and timelines across multiple countries, creates substantial uncertainties at a time when the automotive industry needs stability, certainty and predictability. Policy measures which take a position of technology neutrality, and a market-based approach are most likely to deliver the changes governments say are required. “THE 1.5oC MARKER IS SIGNIFICANT AS A KEY GOAL OF THE 2015 PARIS AGREEMENT – TO TRY TO ENSURE THAT GLOBAL TEMPERATURE CHANGE DOES NOT RISE MORE THAN THIS ABOVE PRE-INDUSTRIAL LEVELS.” Shaping our approach to sustainability ASTON MARTIN LAGONDA SUSTAINABILITY REPORT 2024 8 SHAPING OUR APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY TACKLING CLIMATE CHANGE CREATING A BETTER ENVIRONMENT INVESTING IN PEOPLE RESPONSIBLE BUSINESS FURTHER INFORMATION INTRODUCTION

pages.text: ECONOMIC LANDSCAPE AND GEOPOLITICAL INSTABILITY Slow but stable growth characterised the economic landscape across many major markets in 2024. Inflationary pressures, as highlighted by the World Economic Forum, coupled with supply chain disruptions, continued to create uncertainty. Geopolitical instability is also contributing to supply chain challenges. Conflict in many parts of the world, including in Europe, has a wide-ranging impact on people and businesses. This impact includes issues and disruption arising from trade policy tensions, particularly between the USA, EU, and China. Imposing tariffs on Chinese-made EVs and lithium-ion batteries by the USA and similar tariffs by the EU create added costs and risks of reciprocal trade measures from China, potentially affecting the availability of key components with implications across the automotive supply chain. TRANSPARENT SUPPLY CHAINS Consumers and policymakers increasingly prioritise ethical and transparent supply chains, reflecting heightened scrutiny and public concern over human rights and environmental practices. Reports of forced labour in mining and other activities, especially for materials like cobalt used in batteries, have led to regulatory frameworks such as the UK’s Modern Slavery Act, the EU’s Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive, and the US Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act. NATURE Concerns about the natural environment have become more prominent in the course of 2024, with a mix of insights, demands and actions from civil society organisations, consumers and governments. These concerns, encompassing issues like biodiversity loss, habitat destruction, and deforestation, are another challenge shaping the business environment. The new Global Biodiversity Framework, adopted at the UN Convention on Biological Diversity (COP15), sets ambitious targets to protect natural ecosystems, with governments seeking ways to ensure such commitments become actionable measures. CIRCULARITY A more informed society is reacting to concerns about climate change, nature loss and resource conservation. This is leading to growing expectations for businesses to transition towards circular business models as part of a more circular economy. The end state for circularity emphasises recycling, reuse, and sustainable material choices. Already all Aston Martin cars must meet the EU’s End-of-Life Vehicle regulations, which requires a minimum of 85% of a car to be recyclable, recoverable or reusable. While consumer pressures and government regulations represent two major forces leading to businesses adopting more circular operating models, industry can also benefit from adopting growing levels of circularity, to manage resource use, reduce costs and improve operational efficiencies. CYBERSECURITY AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE Cybersecurity remains an increasingly material risk for companies, threatening operational disruption and reputational damage. Data security is a growing concern amongst consumers and businesses. The rapid evolution and application of Artificial Intelligence (‘AI’) technologies also have potentially far-reaching implications for how businesses operate and their associated societal impacts. This powerful new technology can both assist companies and generate additional risks. As cars become increasingly technology-driven, this is an issue for car makers and our supply chains. pages.text: Materiality Materiality analysis is an important tool for identifying environmental, social and governance priorities; it helps businesses direct sustainability efforts towards areas with the most significant impact and opportunity. 4. ASSESSMENT OF IMPACTS ON SOCIETY AND THE ENVIRONMENT (IMPACT MATERIALITY) Aston Martin subject matter experts rated the severity of impacts on society and the environment based on their scale, scope, irremediability and likelihood. The likelihood rating was also assessed against the Future-Fit Business Benchmark Risk Profiler tool to help ensure a robust and evidence-based assessment of impacts. We then consulted a range of external industry experts to seek their views and validate our assessment of impacts. 5. ASSESSMENT OF RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (FINANCIAL MATERIALITY) The Aston Martin Risk Management team reviewed the risks derived from the climate risk and opportunity assessment and scenario analysis to ensure all key ESG-related financial risks and opportunities were included. The Risk Management team rated the risks and opportunities by magnitude and likelihood, aligning the process with the Company’s Enterprise Risk Management Framework and System (‘ERMFS’). 6. DETERMINATION OF MATERIAL TOPICS IMPACT MATERIALITY: For each impact, the scale, scope and irremediability scores were multiplied together then multiplied by the likelihood of the impact occurring. This produced an impact index score; if the score exceeded a set threshold, the topic was deemed material from an impact perspective. FINANCIAL MATERIALITY: For each risk and opportunity, the magnitude and likelihood scores were multiplied together. This produced a risk/ opportunity index score; if the score exceeded a set threshold, the topic was deemed material from a financial perspective. We categorised the resultant list of material topics into low, medium or high importance to Aston Martin’s stakeholders and shared with the Board Sustainability Committee for discussion against our Racing. Green. strategy and goals. Our full Materiality Assessment Report is available on our website at www.astonmartin.com/corporate/sustainability/sustainability-reports HOW WE IDENTIFIED MATERIAL TOPICS 1. DEVELOPMENT OF RELEVANT SUSTAINABILITY MATTERS We gathered and analysed background research to create a long list of sustainability matters that were potentially material to Aston Martin. For this, we used numerous sources, including previous Aston Martin materiality assessments, ESG raters’ feedback on our response to questionnaires, sustainability reports of peers and industry competitors, global trend reports, sector frameworks, principles and standards, investor perception studies and conversations with supplier partners on their approach to materiality. We scored the topics based on how many times they featured in peer materiality assessments, sector frameworks and standards; then narrowed them down and grouped them into themes, with a description narrative tailored to each stakeholder group to ensure clarity. 2. STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT Having mapped and identified ‘affected stakeholders’, a range of stakeholder groups were engaged to determine the relevance of the sustainability themes and matters to them. This involved meetings with key suppliers and focus groups with employees, customers and community representatives. We asked suppliers to identify risks and opportunities for the sustainability themes (for Aston Martin’s business, as well as for the environment and society), and to score their priority for action by the company. The focus groups were asked which three themes they thought were most important for Aston Martin to focus on, the biggest risks, and the biggest opportunities. 3. LIST OF IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES The 13 sustainability themes and underlying topics identified were mapped to the sustainability topics and subtopics that are included in the Application Requirements (A11 of ESRS 1) of the CSRD and combined with the results and insights from the stakeholder conversations to create a list of potential and actual, negative and positive impacts, as well as financial risks and opportunities associated with each topic. In 2024, we strengthened our approach by implementing a double materiality analysis to determine our material topics. Informed by the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (‘ESRS’) requirements, the analysis helped us understand our material impacts, risks and opportunities. We adopted this approach in anticipation of the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (‘CSRD’). Although Aston Martin is not currently required to report under the CSRD, we recognise the importance of moving in line with best practice. We also moved towards a multi-engagement process with Aston Martin’s stakeholders. Impacts are assessed over the short-, medium-, and long- term, and consider the severity of the impact based on its scale, scope and how difficult it is to remedy. For potential impacts, an assessment of their likelihood is overlaid to determine their significance. FINANCIAL MATERIALITY Financial materiality identifies the importance of sustainability information, risks and opportunities which, if left out, misrepresented or hidden, could reasonably be expected to influence financial decisions. When assessing the materiality of risks and opportunities, both the likelihood of them happening and the potential resulting financial impact on enterprise value (magnitude) are considered. pages.text: Stakeholder engagement The UN Sustainable Development Goals Engagement with stakeholders informs our materiality assessment process. In addition, we engage with our stakeholders through various channels to support our wider approach to sustainability and Company activities, helping us to better understand external developments, and identify opportunities and risks. In 2015, the UN adopted the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development. The UN 2030 agenda identifies 17 sustainable development goals (‘SDGs’) that seek to address the world’s biggest challenges, including ending poverty, improving health, better education, making cities more sustainable and tackling climate change. Governments, businesses and civil society all have important roles to play in achieving the SDGs. Aston Martin has identified eight SDGs that we can directly contribute to, considering the targets and indicators of the individual SDGs, as shown in the table. Indirectly we can also have a positive impact on SDG 3, 4, 11, 13, 14, 15 and 16. GROUP KEY TOPICS, ENGAGEMENT AND ACTION Customers and enthusiasts Our customers and enthusiasts are key to our brand and business success. Customer engagement focuses on the quality, safety, performance and design of our vehicles. We use our luxury customer magazine to communicate on general sustainability topics and in 2024, held two customer focus groups to support the refresh of Racing. Green. The first focused on understanding customer use of our vehicles, feeding into our Scope 3 emissions reporting, the second focus group supported the update of our materiality process. Dealer network Our dealer network reflects the Aston Martin brand beyond our core manufacturing sites, our third-party dealerships are the direct contact point for our brand to our customers. In 2024, we engaged with our UK dealer network to understand their aims and ambitions related to sustainability and the availability of data, particularly to support our net zero target setting. Suppliers and other partnerships Supplier partnerships are fundamental to our business. Responsible procurement with a focus on ethics, transparency and the environment are key topics for our engagement, alongside increasing attention on the environmental and social performance of suppliers. In 2024, we launched a sustainability supplier assessment questionnaire, engaged suppliers on emissions data and ambition and involved several Tier 1 suppliers in the development of our materiality process. The outcome of this engagement is supporting the strategic embedding of sustainability into our procurement processes. Our people Our people are the key to our success. Personal development, health and safety, Equity, Diversity and Inclusion (‘EDI’), and sustainability are key themes highlighted as important through our engagement. More than 2,100 employees (78% of eligible employees) participated in our 2024 Great Place to Work® employee survey providing feedback on workplace culture and satisfaction. We held town hall meetings and roundtables as well as launching our new employee communication platform. The outcomes support the development of key programmes such as employee wellbeing, as well as embedding Racing. Green. across the business. Investors Our focus is to ensure investors understand our strategy, value drivers, performance, ambition and culture and for us to understand their priorities. Sustainability, governance and transparency matter to them along with other key financial and business priorities. In addition to core engagement activities, we’ve undertaken direct meetings focused on sustainability with specific investors. Local communities and NGOs We aim to build positive relationships with local communities and organisations interested in our business. Local engagement and our materiality process show that ethics, safety, career opportunities and our local impact are important topics. In 2024, we rolled out our Community Investment Policy setting out our approach to charitable giving, we continued our schools outreach activities and took part in local community forums. This has provided key feedback into the refresh of our sustainability strategy and increased our focus on how we strategically support our local communities. Government and regulators We engage with government and regulators given public policy and regulatory impacts on our business. We aim to engage constructively and consistently through various channels, including roundtables, site visits, contributing to public policy development and responding to consultations. Transparency and political neutrality are at the heart of our engagement. We have identified public policy-related risks and opportunities, drafting internal reports for our Executive Committee on geo-political developments and considering how these impact on our sustainability strategy. For more information on how we engage with our stakeholders see pages 16–19 in our Annual Report and our Stakeholder Engagement Report, which is available on our website at www.astonmartin.com/corporate/sustainability/sustainability-reports. GREEN. Aston Martin strives to be a responsible business, protecting the environment and making a positive contribution to society by creating jobs and driving innovation and opportunity. We manage sustainability through effective governance, risk management, compliance, including transparent and robust reporting, and collaboration and building strong partnerships across the value chain based on respect for human rights. We recognise that progress with sustainability requires an integrated approach with effort required in all areas of our business and across our entire value chain. Our sustainability strategy, Racing. Green., outlines our vision to becoming a world-leading sustainable ultra-luxury automotive business. The strategy is based on a clear understanding of the priorities of our customers, employees and wider stakeholder groups and represents an integrated approach focused on three key pillars: ‘Tackling climate change’, ‘Creating a better environment’, and ‘Investing in people’. Each pillar includes clear targets, supported by our commitment to operate as a ‘Responsible business’. We are committed to complying with all relevant regulations, and aligning with international standards in areas such as environmental management, biodiversity and safe working practices. In 2024, we reviewed and updated Racing. Green., taking account of inputs from customers, supply chain partners, employees, shareholders and other stakeholders. We also completed several major enabling projects, including updating our climate risk scenarios to understand how future climate changes might impact our business and its wider supply chain, reviewing human rights risks, and developing a credible net zero target in line with the Science Based Targets initiative (‘SBTi’) Net-Zero Standard, supported by a robust plan for how we will transition to net zero. This target has been submitted to the SBTi for validation. On pages 57–59 we have included a performance update against the goals, targets and commitments of our previous strategy. Several targets are no longer included in our updated strategy, which was approved by the Board in December 2024. Racing. Green. pages.text: Sustainability Committee Report 2024 DR. ANNE STEVENS INDEPENDENT NON-EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR CHAIR OF THE BOARD SUSTAINABILITY COMMITTEE The Board Committee met four times in the course of the year, with additional engagement with the wider Aston Martin Board on the Company’s decarbonisation strategy and the overall strategic priorities for Racing. Green. We received reports across our meetings with safety performance being an ever-present topic and where improvements are continuing. We also considered sustainability performance across a wide range of other topics and had in-depth reviews on priority areas such as supply chain sustainability, human rights and new sustainability related legislation. Sustainability is being embraced at every level in the organisation, with employees initiating and delivering sustainability activities. The breadth of action runs from topics around EDI, through to evolving processes to drive efficiency and embracing innovation to deliver world-class products. The strengthening of the Company’s core sustainability team in 2024, those dedicated personnel who serve as vital strategists and enablers to drive progress, has also been another positive step forward. We continue to build our expertise and delivery capabilities in all areas of sustainability from human rights to responsible sourcing, life cycle assessment and CO2 management. This was essential action to ensure we can realise Aston Martin’s sustainability ambitions. A key element of the updated Racing. Green. strategy is climate change, the predominant sustainability priority. In 2024, the Company submitted net zero targets to the SBTi for validation. Our approach was founded on a thorough investigation of the Company’s Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions data and plans. The Board Committee was fully engaged, including undertaking dedicated meetings to evaluate relevant data, operational insights and the proposed decarbonisation strategy in-depth. Sustainability and circularity principles are now starting to be incorporated more formally into the Company’s product design and development process, and there has been an intensified focus on the wider value chain. An updated Responsible Procurement Policy has been introduced to set clear expectations to Suppliers on sustainability. A commitment to high-performance and best practices is reflected in efforts by the Company proactively developing new partnerships to facilitate collaboration on sustainability. For example, joining Drive Sustainability, an initiative that brings together leading automotive companies, means our teams can take learning and best practice from across the sector. The Company also continues to work with partners to drive new technical innovation in areas such as lightweighting and the use of lower CO2 materials. There is more to do, but Aston Martin is clear on the way forward. Success will depend on many factors including global macroeconomic and geopolitical headwinds, volatility and uncertainty. Even in this context however, we remain resolute and we continue on a journey into the future with sustainability increasingly at the heart of all we do. I am pleased to again present the report on behalf of the Sustainability Committee. In 2024, Aston Martin continued to strengthen its commitment to sustainability. The Company made advances in understanding its wider environmental and societal impacts through improved data gathering and analysis, as well as gaining further insights into the expectations of its stakeholders. These actions have shaped the update to the Company’s sustainability strategy, Racing. Green. and put the Company in a stronger position to take action to continue to improve its sustainability performance, including working with value chain partners and other stakeholders. The Board Sustainability Committee continues to oversee the evolution and execution of Racing. Green., offering guidance, challenge and support. Jean Tomlin OBE joined the Board Committee in 2024, bringing a wealth of experience in people and organisational culture to further strengthen and expand the Committee’s capabilities and expertise. Adrian Hallmark, our new CEO, attended the Board Sustainability Committee upon his appointment to Aston Martin. He adds further strength in the Company’s sustainability expertise given his substantial achievements at Bentley where he was the driving force behind that Company’s highly successful sustainability programme. pages.text: OUR TARGETS REDUCING GREENHOUSE GAS EMISSIONS Reduce absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 GHG emissions 42% by 2030, from a 2022 base year Reduce absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 GHG emissions 90% by 2050, from a 2022 base year 90% BY 2050 42% BY 2030 Aiming for net zero Climate change is a vital global sustainability issue. The UN Paris Agreement created a legally binding international treaty to limit global warming below 2°C and striving for a 1.5°C limit. However, in 2024 the UN reported that 2023 saw the highest levels of GHG emissions on record, while wildfires, heatwaves, storms, and droughts intensified globally. The World Meteorological Organisation reported that 2024 was the hottest year on record, exceeding 1.5°C above the pre-industrial average. Despite this, the UN confirm, limiting average global warming below 1.5°C remains technically possible but acceleration is needed and is a critical challenge for governments and business. Our updated sustainability strategy underscores our commitment to play our part in accelerating emission reductions and managing the impacts of climate change on our business. To achieve this, we will:  Deliver our Net Zero plan, transforming our manufacturing operations and products, and decarbonise our value chain.  Embed a risk-based approach to identify, assess, and manage climate-related risks and opportunities. We have set ambitious near-term and longer-term net zero GHG emission reduction targets aligned with the Paris Agreement, which we have submitted to the SBTi for validation. These targets replace our previous emissions and energy reduction goals. The targets are supported within our business by robust action plans, and we will continue to monitor and report our progress annually, including continuing to report through CDP (formerly the Carbon Disclosure Project) ensuring transparency for our stakeholders. “WE HAVE SET AMBITIOUS NEAR-TERM AND LONGER-TERM NET ZERO GHG EMISSION REDUCTION TARGETS ALIGNED WITH THE PARIS AGREEMENT, WHICH WE HAVE SUBMITTED TO THE SBTi FOR VALIDATION.” ASTON MARTIN LAGONDA SUSTAINABILITY REPORT 2024 17 SHAPING OUR APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY TACKLING CLIMATE CHANGE CREATING A BETTER ENVIRONMENT INVESTING IN PEOPLE RESPONSIBLE BUSINESS FURTHER INFORMATION INTRODUCTION

pages.text: TOTAL EMISSIONS FOOTPRINT 2023 Scope 1 1% Scope 2, Market-based <1% Scope 3 99% 1,153,127 TONNES CO2e Our emissions Our emissions footprint is calculated and broken down into three categories, based on the GHG Protocol: SCOPE 1: Direct emissions from sources that are owned or controlled by Aston Martin. We are focused on minimising emissions from our own operations. The leading source of our Scope 1 emissions is natural gas, which we utilise for both our heating and our paint operations. SCOPE 2: Indirect emissions from the consumption of purchased energy. Our Scope 2 emissions are minimal, according to the market-based accounting method, because of our ongoing commitment to procuring 100% renewable electricity backed by Renewable Energy Guarantees of Origin at our manufacturing sites. SCOPE 3: Other indirect emissions that occur in the Company’s value chain, both upstream and downstream. Our Scope 3 emissions are more than 99% of our total emissions. These emissions predominantly result from the use of our sold products and from our purchased goods and services. Decarbonising our own operations To achieve our ambition of reducing our emissions in our own operations (Scope 1 and 2) by 42% by 2030, from a 2022 baseline, we have developed an overarching decarbonisation strategy. We will continue to refine and develop this strategy through 2025 and beyond as needed. Our 2024 Scope 1 and 2 emissions have broadly stayed the same since 2022. Our Scope 1 and 2 reduction approach includes three key decarbonisation levers, the first focused on energy efficiency, the second on emission reduction through renewable electricity purchase and production, and the third on electrification of our operations. In developing our net zero targets, we worked extensively with external consultants to identify the potential opportunities across our sites. This road map gives us an initial view of critical actions needed to start longer-term electrification projects as well as short-term actions. The following is an example of the types of activity currently being considered under each lever: LEVER 1 ENERGY EFFICIENCY  Continued roll out of LED lighting and optimising building controls  Completion of sub-metering  Insulation and heat recovery  Reducing compressed-air use LEVER 2 ELECTRICITY PURCHASE AND PRODUCTION  Continue projects for solar PV at St Athan and Gaydon  Consider energy contracts at leased sites LEVER 3 ELECTRIFICATION  Decarbonise production processes and space heating  Installation of heats pumps Eleven of our 12 sites in the UK, including our two main manufacturing sites at Gaydon and St Athan, are powered by 100% renewable electricity using supplies backed by Renewable Energy Guarantees of Origin. We continue to look to increase our self-generation and reduce our dependency on the national electricity distribution network. In 2023, we completed the installation of solar PV generation at Newport Pagnell and continue to aim to install solar PV generation at our main manufacturing sites at St Athan and Gaydon. Our 2024 emissions for Scope 1 were 8,574.81 tCO2e and for Scope 2 were 599.49 tCO2e (market-based) and 7,160.25 tCO2e (location-based). Our full data set including our data in line with the UK Streamlined Energy and Carbon Reporting Regulations (‘SECR’), can be found on page 54. The first supported the refinement of our Scope 3 data, focused on our customers’ views on how Aston Martin vehicles are driven, what the difference between heritage and modern cars may mean for driving patterns and end-of-life vehicles. Later in the year, we hosted another group of customers to hear which sustainability topics are important to them and what they consider important to Aston Martin. Their insights helped shape our materiality process and outcomes. “WE HOSTED A GROUP OF CUSTOMERS AT OUR GAYDON SITE TO SHARE OUR INITIAL SCOPE 3 BASELINE. THEIR INSIGHTS INTO ASTON MARTIN DRIVING PATTERNS AND HERITAGE VEHICLES HELPED US REFINE OUR DATA AND ASSUMPTIONS.” RHIAN WALTERS, SUSTAINABILITY ADVISER Decarbonising our value chain We calculated our full Scope 3 emissions for the first time in 2023, using a 2022 baseline, as published in our 2023 Sustainability Report. We have yet to disclose our 2024 Scope 3 emissions because of the significant data requirements and time needed to secure this data from the wider value chain. Throughout 2024, we have worked to refine these figures, which now serve as the baseline for all targets and future results. We focused on our largest emission areas, expanding the share of emissions covered by actual data as opposed to spend-based estimations, and improving our modelling on the lifetime emissions of the cars sold in the reporting year. In our upstream and downstream value chain we have identified nine key levers that support the achievement of our 42% reduction by 2030 and net zero by 2050 targets. These align to the core impact areas for our Scope 3 emissions which together account for over 95% of our Scope 3 emissions: Use of sold products, Purchased good and services, Capital goods, Upstream transportation and distribution. pages.text: USE OF SOLD PRODUCTS The largest contributor to our Scope 3 emissions is from the use of the cars sold in the reporting year (Category 11: Use of sold products). We anticipate that these emissions will increase in the short-term, predominantly due to increases in the volume of cars sold per year. However, as our transition to PHEV and then BEV kicks in towards 2030, we expect emissions will decrease at the pace needed to meet our targets. * from 2023 to 2027 PURCHASED GOODS AND SERVICES AND CAPITAL GOODS Purchased goods and services and Capital goods account for around 38% of the Company’s 2023 Scope 3 emissions. To enhance the underlying data and to engage suppliers on emission reduction efforts, we took a targeted approach, surveying the companies representing 70% of emissions among our direct suppliers. We have integrated supplier-specific emissions data where available into our calculations. We will engage further with suppliers who can provide product-level carbon footprints, which we plan to integrate into our Scope 3 footprint. Our procurement policies are designed to help us reduce emissions across the supply chain. Our Responsible Procurement Policy, updated in 2024, requires suppliers to have an environmental management system in place to identify, measure and mitigate GHG emissions, provide product-level GHG emissions data, including from sub-suppliers, logistics and other activities upon request. UPSTREAM AND DOWNSTREAM LOGISTICS Logistics comprises around 2% of our 2023 Scope 3 emissions. This is an important area of focus, working in partnership with our logistic providers to optimise the transport of components and finished vehicles to and from our manufacturing sites. Our short-term priorities include transitioning our inbound fleet to carbon-reducing fuels, optimising transport frequency to eliminate inefficiencies and rethinking our supply chain footprint through centralised approaches like near-shoring and reducing miles travelled. As part of our Advanced Propulsion Centre funded project, ELEVATION, we have designed tools to support our supply chain teams, as part of developing a net zero supply chain tactical plan and assessment. Our approach to carbon neutrality and carbon offsetting DEFINING NET ZERO AND CARBON NEUTRAL Aston Martin uses the following definitions: Net zero is achieved when a company reduces its value chain GHG emissions to near-zero (defined as at least 90% reduction) in line with the goal of limiting global temperature rise to 1.5°C and permanently neutralises any residual emissions at the net zero target year. This definition is aligned with that used by the SBTi. Carbon neutrality is achieved when a company’s activities result in no net increase in global GHG emissions over a specific period, often by offsetting emissions through carbon credit purchases (although permanent neutralisation is not required). This definition is aligned with the PAS 2060 standard. In our previous sustainability strategy, we committed to carbon neutral manufacturing facilities. Working with the Carbon Trust, in 2023 Aston Martin Lagonda Ltd and Aston Martin Works Ltd were certified as carbon neutral for 2022 emissions in accordance with PAS 2060. The Carbon Trust no longer offers carbon neutral certification based on evolving stakeholder consensus and regulatory frameworks around sustainability; as a result, the Company decided not to pursue further carbon neutral certification in 2024. In submitting our net zero targets to the SBTi we have made a clear commitment to emissions reductions. However, we recognise that as we move along our reduction pathway, we still have an emissions impact. In line with our previous commitment to carbon neutrality, we continue to purchase high-integrity carbon credits to deliver immediate emissions reductions while also supporting projects with wider societal benefit. In 2024, we purchased verified credits aligned with our 2023 Scope 1 and 2 (market-based) emissions. These credits support gas leak reduction efforts in Bangladesh, reducing the leakage of methane emissions but also training local people to improve the network directly. Alongside this we are reviewing the SBTi’s approach to beyond value chain mitigation and in 2024 purchased additional carbon credits to support afforestation projects in the UK verified by the Woodland Carbon Code. We continue to comply with requirements to purchase carbon credits to meet varying tail pipe emissions standards legislation across different countries. 70% WE TOOK A TARGETED APPROACH, SURVEYING THE COMPANIES REPRESENTING 70% OF EMISSIONS AMONG OUR DIRECT SUPPLIERS. “WE ARE INVESTING C.£2BN IN OUR LONG-TERM GROWTH AND THE TRANSITION TO ELECTRIFICATION.”* ASTON MARTIN LAGONDA SUSTAINABILITY REPORT 2024 20 SHAPING OUR APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY TACKLING CLIMATE CHANGE CREATING A BETTER ENVIRONMENT INVESTING IN PEOPLE RESPONSIBLE BUSINESS FURTHER INFORMATION INTRODUCTION

pages.text: Climate risks and opportunities Climate change is one of the most significant risks that organisations face. Experts widely recognise that global warming could lead to damaging economic and social consequences, although the exact timing and severity of the physical effects are difficult to estimate. The large-scale and long-term nature of the problem makes it uniquely challenging, especially in the context of economic decision-making. We continue to advance our understanding of climate-related risks and opportunities, developing our climate-related scenarios and integrating them within our ERMFS. CLIMATE SCENARIO ANALYSIS In 2021, Aston Martin undertook its first climate risk scenario analysis and we have reported annually in line with TCFD requirements. This year, in line with best practice, we have undertaken a refresh of our climate risk scenarios and developed our focus on physical risks. Key areas of focus in the refresh have included:  Identifying physical and transition risks and opportunities  Understanding our business strategy resilience within a range of plausible futures through qualitative scenario analysis  Financial quantification pilots on two transition risks The outcomes from this work and a fuller explanation of our climate risks and opportunities are included in our TCFD report on pages 67 to 73. pages.text: Creating a better environment We recognise that alongside climate change we have wider responsibilities to protect the environment. Our aim is to maximise resource efficiency and deliver net positive biodiversity at our two main manufacturing sites. We are working to better understand the life cycle impacts of our vehicles and operations. As a responsible business we are committed to addressing challenges in the natural world including a range of interconnected environmental issues, such as climate change, biodiversity loss, habitat destruction and deforestation. pages.text: OUR TARGETS Improve biodiversity year-on-year at our manufacturing sites (measured by Biodiversity Index Score) 0 Zero waste to landfill Reduce the amount of waste per car built by 3% each year 3% 30% reduction in water consumption per car by 2030 30% Operational efficiency Industry is transitioning from linear to circular business models, utilising products and materials many times over and designing, producing and consuming in a way that minimises the use of resources, cuts waste and reduces carbon emissions. Alongside this, over half of worldwide GDP is highly or moderately dependent on natural ecosystems. The UN has reported that the natural environment is declining at an unprecedented rate, and human actions have significantly altered the land and marine environments. To respond to this situation, the UN has called for transformative changes in society and industry. Aston Martin’s updated sustainability strategy focuses on minimising our impacts on the natural world. This includes eliminating and minimising pollution, waste and use of resources in all our operations to reduce the environmental impact from our products along their life cycle. Environmental management Our Environmental Policy sets out our commitment to protecting the environment, and to ensure we fulfil our environmental compliance obligations. Our Policy is available on our website at www.astonmartin.com/corporate/sustainability/policies. Our operational environment team has responsibility for our ISO 14001:2015 environmental management system certification and work with external environmental protection bodies to ensure compliance. We have achieved ISO 14001:2015 at our Gaydon and St Athan sites and the Company is working towards ISO 50001 (energy management) as part of our overall approach to environmental management at our main manufacturing facilities. We aim to have a Stage 1 ISO 50001 audit early in 2025 and will proceed towards full certification later in the year. Waste Our 2024 materiality assessment identified waste as a material topic, considering both the environmental impact and business risk due to increasing regulatory compliance around waste from production and the potential impact linked to vehicle end-of-life. We aim to minimise waste and target zero waste to landfill. We continue to build a clearer view of where and why waste is generated across our value chain. In 2024, we saw a 27% increase in waste levels at our manufacturing sites per car built compared to 2023. As a result, we engaged specialist consultants to map our waste streams in greater detail, to improve waste management and reduction planning. At our Gaydon and St Athan sites we use a specialist contractor to manage our waste activities from on-site monitoring, segregation and management through to waste disposal. This contractor also undertakes relevant training as needed with Aston Martin staff. In 2024, we generated 3478.34 tonnes of waste at our UK sites, a 23% increase from 2023. We sent no waste to landfill from our UK sites, however we have identified a small volume of waste from Newport Pagnell where the consignment note does not categorise final waste destination as non-landfill at the point of reporting. We are focused on understanding where, why and how to mitigate waste arising to support our waste reduction target. Air quality Air quality is a material topic because of the potentially negative impact of air pollutants from paint and solvents used in vehicle production. We operate several processes under permit to the relevant local authority, predominately linked to our paint operations, which set air quality requirements for particulate matter, Volatile Organic Compounds (‘VOCs’), Carbon Monoxide (‘CO’) and Nitrous Oxides (‘NOx’). We actively manage our operations to ensure compliance with permits and to minimise any pollutants. We engage an external third party to undertake monitoring on our behalf and in line with the Monitoring Certification Scheme for Equipment, a certification scheme for environmental monitoring personnel, organisations and equipment in the UK. pages.text: BIODIVERSITY SCORE 2023 2024 Gaydon St Athan 82 86 88 90 84 80 Water Our main water demand is generated by our manufacturing processes at our St Athan and Gaydon sites, although our operations are not regarded as water intensive. According to the World Resource Institute Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas, Gaydon is in an area of low-medium water stress and St Athan is in an area of low stress. We work with the relevant water companies for our sites to manage any local water resource management concerns that may occur. We have continued to improve the monitoring of water usage during 2024 and upgraded sanitary fixtures to improve water efficiency as part of the refurbishment of Gaydon. Water consumption at our UK sites accounts for 94% of the Company’s total water consumption and is supplied by regional water companies; we do not self-abstract. We return our wastewater to the foul sewers (based on the relevant consent) for treatment by the relevant regional water company. We continuously work to ensure that materials and chemicals remain separated, and that pollution of water resources does not occur. In line with our consent requirements, we undertake direct sampling with the relevant environmental protection authority at least annually. 22% IN 2024, WE CONSUMED 51,428.79 M3 OF WATER, A 22% DECREASE FROM 2023. To ensure we continue to take ownership of our own water resource management, we have set a target in our updated Racing. Green. strategy to reduce total water consumption at manufacturing sites by 30% per car by 2030 against a 2022 baseline. We are currently making good progress towards this target, having delivered an overall reduction of 16% against the baseline. Biodiversity Our operational biodiversity approach is focused on our main sites at Gaydon and St Athan. Together these sites have around 16.4 hectares of green space including our nature trail at Gaydon, the equivalent of about 22 football pitches. This green space provides a variety of habitats, including areas of species rich grassland, hedgerows, mature trees, drainage ditches and disturbed ground, all of which have high wildlife values. We strive to improve the biodiversity on our sites and to minimise ecological impacts, carrying out specialist risk assessments and monitoring prior to undertaking activities that may impact biodiversity on our sites. In 2023, we implemented biodiversity management plans at Gaydon and St Athan and we monitor against these using the UK Governments Department of Food and Rural Affairs (‘DEFRA’) Biodiversity Metric 1.03 ecological baseline condition assessment methodology. Action taken in 2024 included:  Monitoring protected species activity  Wildflower seeding  Thinning woodland areas to encourage wildflower growth  Clearing ground scrub from newt habitats (ponds)  Creating insect habitats As reported by the UN, we are also seeing the impact of a global decline in pollinators. We have noted a reduction in bee numbers at our hives, which were installed in Gaydon in 2022, as the bees struggle to find sufficient foraging areas. We are working with the Warwickshire Beekeepers Association to identify how we can support our bee population in an increasingly challenging natural environment, including ensuring the land we have available has the right early blooming species at critical times in the year. pages.text: BUILDING A SUSTAINABILITY-LED CULTURE We continued our Sustainability-led employee communications and engagement activities through 2024 to further embed Racing. Green. Both the Sustainability team and other functions lead these communications, which are aligned with our sustainability strategy. We held two employee focus groups as part of our updated materiality process, which also provided feedback on employees’ thoughts on Racing. Green. and how we can involve them further. In September, we launched a ‘conversational’ webinar series with employees whose roles cross over with elements of Racing. Green. with topics including our EV transformation, EDI and what being an ally means, and we will continue this into 2025. Our new employee engagement platform, Workvivo has enabled swifter communication with our colleagues, and we have seen excellent engagement, particularly around our community involvement activities. Beyond our operations Water scarcity and dependency on nature are two key upstream value chain issues. The UN reports that around half of the world’s population currently experiences severe water scarcity for at least part of the year, we therefore recognise the importance of addressing water scarcity. Alongside this, the automotive sector has been ranked in the top 20 sectors most dependant on nature for direct and supply chain gross value added. We are developing our strategy in these areas and will add targets in the future as we progress our approach. Water (upstream impacts) We concentrate our focus on water resource management at our operational sites but remain aware of the wider impacts of our extended value chain and in particular upstream activities on global water resources. The importance of this issue was highlighted in our materiality assessment. As part of our climate scenario refresh, we selected key sites across our value chain based on physical climate risks and where we anticipated significant impact on the Company’s ability to operate. This identified an increasing trend over time of water stress within specific supply chain areas, whilst a decreasing trend for risk on our own operational sites. In 2025, we will investigate these results further, and develop contingency plans as required. Nature The Taskforce for Nature-related Financial Disclosures (‘TNFD’) is a market- led, science-based and government-supported global initiative. The TNFD provides recommendations and guidance to organisations to encourage and enable them to assess and act on evolving nature-related dependencies, impacts, risks and opportunities. In 2024, Aston Martin joined the TNFD Forum, a platform for organisations to signal their support, contribute to the further development of guidance, and to learn from others through pilot testing and focus groups. The Forum helps the Company access best practice on protecting the environment, improve risk management, and meeting new reporting standards. In our 2024 materiality assessment, biodiversity and ecosystems were identified as a material topic due to Aston Martin’s potentially negative impact on the environment caused by the extraction and processing of materials we use, including steel, aluminium, and plastic. Through 2025, we will look at how we can further embed reporting and map nature risks to better understand our impacts, risks and opportunities. Getting our employees involved Our employees play an active role in supporting efforts to improve the environment and we have a network of environmental champions who help embed environmental thinking within the business as well as identify opportunities for improvement. In 2024, activities that employees have been involved with include:  Wildflower seed bombing  Running litter picking sessions  Hosting webinars on environmental topics  Removing disposable cups from satellite offices  Reducing waste through process improvements in parts ordering ASTON MARTIN LAGONDA SUSTAINABILITY REPORT 2024 25 SHAPING OUR APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY TACKLING CLIMATE CHANGE CREATING A BETTER ENVIRONMENT INVESTING IN PEOPLE RESPONSIBLE BUSINESS FURTHER INFORMATION INTRODUCTION

pages.text: At Aston Martin we are constantly exploring new ways to use materials to manufacture our products, with new innovations introduced as we develop and launch new vehicles through our product cycles. A core focus of our sustainable innovation approach remains vehicle efficiency, circularity, choice of materials and developing the right partnerships to support our pursuit of innovation. The development of our new vehicles follows a programme management approach that takes a vehicle from conception through to full-scale production. In 2024, we updated this approach to ensure that we factor sustainability into the process early on, keep it embedded at all relevant delivery stages and link it to reducing our main Scope 3 emissions impacts. SUSTAINABLE MATERIALS Our materiality assessment identified sustainable materials as material due to the opportunity to significantly reduce Aston Martin’s Scope 3 emissions and the positive environmental impact of conserving natural resources. It was also identified as a material financial opportunity due to supply chain risks. Sustainability is a key focus for our design teams. We review our products against high standards of quality and durability as well as key sustainability attributes such as recyclability, supply chain traceability, carbon and wider environmental impacts. For example, all wood used in our vehicle models is Forest Stewardship Council (‘FSC’) certified, which confirms that the wood comes from forests that are managed to preserve biodiversity and uphold forest management principles that benefit the lives of local people and workers. Sustainable design OUR ST ATHAN MANUFACTURING SITE COMBATING DEFORESTATION Forests provide solutions to global challenges. Whilst the UN report that deforestation is slowing, forests are under pressure from climate-related stressors and forest product demand is growing. As a by-product of the cattle industry, leather and deforestation are often linked. Aston Martin’s main supplier of leather products, used to craft the ultra-luxury interiors in our vehicles is a UK-based company, Bridge of Weir. Bridge of Weir has committed to the absence of deforestation in their supply chain. Their ability to track and trace from farm to finished product allows Bridge of Weir to prove the absence of deforestation. All their raw hides are a by-product of the local beef and dairy industries, from which they have 100% traceability. Sourcing raw hides locally within the UK and Ireland from responsible suppliers, without risk of deforestation, transparently and with full traceability provide Aston Martin with the confirmation of guaranteed provenance without deforestation. “WE DELIVER FULL SUPPLY CHAIN TRANSPARENCY WITH 100% TRACEABILITY AND 0% DEFORESTATION BY SOURCING 100% BY-PRODUCT OF THE FOOD INDUSTRY.” DR WARREN BOWDEN, HEAD OF SUSTAINABILITY & INNOVATION, SCOTTISH LEATHER GROUP ASTON MARTIN LAGONDA SUSTAINABILITY REPORT 2024 26 SHAPING OUR APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY TACKLING CLIMATE CHANGE CREATING A BETTER ENVIRONMENT INVESTING IN PEOPLE RESPONSIBLE BUSINESS FURTHER INFORMATION INTRODUCTION

pages.text: VEHICLE END-OF-LIFE, RECYCLING AND RE-MANUFACTURING In our 2024 materiality assessment, end-of-life vehicles was identified as a material topic due to the positive impacts remanufacturing and prolonging the life of our vehicles can offer our customers, the environment and society. It was also identified as a material topic due to the potential negative environmental impact of end-of-life pollution. Longevity, vehicle recycling and remanufacturing were regarded by stakeholders as being of high importance. We are dedicated to quality and craftmanship over the whole life of our vehicles, supporting vehicle longevity. We estimate that 95% of vehicles ever manufactured by Aston Martin are still ‘on the road’ but vehicle end-of-life and minimising associated environmental impacts are important parts of our design process. Whilst we currently have many elements of a circular approach to business, we are investigating and developing the most appropriate, overarching strategy which looks at keeping our vehicles on the road as long as possible, promoting material circularity and improving reuse of waste onsite through operations. We use the International Material Data System (‘IMDS’), the automotive industry’s material data system. In IMDS, all materials used for automotive manufacturing are collected, maintained, analysed and archived. Using IMDS in collaboration with all AML suppliers, we have full transparency of vehicle content at the design and manufacturing stages. PARTNERING FOR INNOVATION Pioneering innovation is often driven by collaboration and partnerships. In 2023, the Company was awarded government funding to supplement the research and development of Aston Martin’s innovative modular BEV platform. The funding is part of a £9m government grant from the Advanced Propulsion Centre (‘APC’) for Project ELEVATION, a six-partner collaborative research and development project led by Aston Martin, and supported by the Manufacturing Technology Centre, Expert Tooling & Automation, Creative Composites, Fuzzy Logic Studio and WMG, University of Warwick. The project addresses the technical challenges of developing a lightweight, 800V traction battery pack and twin front electric drive unit into a modular BEV platform with a bandwidth from supercar to SUV and includes investment in vehicle light weighting, a digital toolchain and electrification training. This year the project has:  Identified a 26% mass saving for composite concept over baseline high voltage battery enclosure design  Mapped the manufacturing digital landscape with a transformation roadmap  Developed a net zero supply chain tactical plan, assessments and tools  Delivered core functionality for an integrated 3D immersive training platform In 2024, we joined with several partners to secure a £6m, government- funded innovation project supporting the development of lightweight, sustainable aluminium castings for our vehicles led by aluminium supplier, Sarginsons Industries. PROJECT PIVOT Supported by Innovate UK and the APC, Project PIVOT (‘Performance Integrated Vehicle Optimisation Technology’) aims to revolutionise sustainable vehicle manufacturing by creating lighter, more sustainable cast aluminium components. Led by Sarginsons Industries, PIVOT brings together Aston Martin, Brunel University London, GESCRAP and Altair. The project will develop innovative software that will result in cast aluminium structures that are up to 30% lighter while maintaining performance and using 100% recycled aluminium to reduce embedded carbon emissions in vehicle components by up to 95%. Advanced grain refinement techniques developed in PIVOT will make it possible to use secondary alloys for cast structural applications such as body structures and vehicle chassis systems to reduce mass and embedded carbon in future Aston Martin vehicles. “WE ARE DELIGHTED TO BE PUSHING BOUNDARIES IN TERMS OF SUPERCAR PERFORMANCE WITH ASTON MARTIN, WHILST ALSO LEADING THE INCREASE IN SUSTAINABILITY OF THE ALUMINIUM CASTING INDUSTRY. IT WILL BE A TRUE GAME CHANGER.” ASHLEY STAFFORD, COMMERCIAL DIRECTOR, SARGINSONS ASTON MARTIN LAGONDA SUSTAINABILITY REPORT 2024 28 SHAPING OUR APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY TACKLING CLIMATE CHANGE CREATING A BETTER ENVIRONMENT INVESTING IN PEOPLE RESPONSIBLE BUSINESS FURTHER INFORMATION INTRODUCTION

pages.text: Investing in people Our aim is to provide a safe, diverse, equitable and inclusive workplace, where our colleagues are supported to meet their goals and aspirations and can make positive, lasting impact by collaborating with our local and international communities to support wider society. Our People Strategy has been developed to accelerate progress towards a world-class employee experience. We deliver our strategy through four pillars: Organisation Capability, Culture, People and Talent Development and HR Service Delivery to the Company. Our approach to our values and promoting a diverse and inclusive workforce applies across all these pillars. TO ACHIEVE THIS WE WILL:  Create an environment that enables a positive work-life experience, valuing safety, health and mental wellbeing  Provide purposeful employment for all our employees in a diverse and inclusive workplace  Build skills that support long-term employability and our transition to electrification  Maintain social investment in our communities to support sustainable development aligned with local needs The results of our 2024 double materiality assessment highlighted the following material topics that sit under our ‘Investing in People’ pillar:  Investing in young people (early careers and STEM)  Employee EDI, training and skills  Employee health and safety IN THIS SECTION  Culture, safety, health and wellbeing  Equity, diversity and inclusion  Skills  Society OUR KEY ACTIVITY  Developed and launched Community Investment Policy  Launch of new employee engagement platform  Increased employee-led EDI activity and awareness  All employee engagement on safe working practices UN SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS 86% Of employees feel proud to work for Aston Martin 26% Increase in training hours compared to 2023 12.5% Improvement in Accident Frequency Rate compared to 2023 ASTON MARTIN LAGONDA SUSTAINABILITY REPORT 2024 30 SHAPING OUR APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY TACKLING CLIMATE CHANGE CREATING A BETTER ENVIRONMENT INVESTING IN PEOPLE RESPONSIBLE BUSINESS FURTHER INFORMATION INTRODUCTION

pages.text: OUR TARGETS Improve workplace engagement and culture, and secure accreditation as a Great Place To Work® by 2025 Zero accidents in our business 0 OUR VALUES Our values set the tone for how we do things and the culture we want to establish. This is supported by our Code of Conduct. We have rolled out values training to almost 2,800 people across our business since 2023. Our values are: UNITY OPENNESS TRUST OWNERSHIP COURAGE At the core of our values is one single guiding tenet: NO ONE BUILDS AN ASTON MARTIN ON THEIR OWN Culture, safety, health and wellbeing People are at the heart of our business and the communities we operate in globally. Our updated sustainability strategy continues our commitment to a workplace and wider society where people are empowered. We want to ensure our employees feel they have a safe and great place to come to work every day. We are targeting zero accidents and aim to be recognised as a Great Place To Work® by 2025. Prioritising safety In our 2024 materiality assessment, employee health and safety was identified as a material topic. Prioritising safety is a vital part of working as an ethical organisation and at the core of protecting employee wellbeing. Aston Martin’s ambition is to achieve zero accidents across its business. In 2024, the Company’s Accident Frequency Rate (‘AFR’) was 0.35. SAFETY MANAGEMENT Our safety management system is designed to maintain the highest standards of safety and welfare for all employees, business partners and visitors and is aligned to ISO 45001. It follows the ‘plan, do, check, act’ model to continually improve all related policies, procedures and working instructions. Supporting this, we have been investing in new technology and safety systems to enhance and improve incident recording, safety trend analysis and the management and closure of incidents which have helped improve safety performance. We are audited by the British Standards Institute (‘BSI’), with our Gaydon manufacturing site audited annually. BSI’s approach includes best-practice safety indicators: leadership; stakeholder engagement; risk management; organisational health and safety culture; continual improvement; and wellbeing. RISK IDENTIFICATION In 2024, we enhanced our ability to identify safety-related risks by working with employees to develop new safety training tailored to their specific operational environments. This uniform and consistent approach has allowed us to improve the quality and standards of risk assessments ensuring all trained individuals have the same level of understanding and ability to identify hazards. Further courses will be developed in 2025 tailored to individual business functions improving the pro-active identification of hazards and risks. We are rolling out a similar approach to ergonomics, improving the quality and standard of ergonomic risk assessments, and ensuring a consistent approach is used to understand and identify ergonomic hazards. SAFETY PERFORMANCE Health and safety performance is monitored at monthly health and safety committee meetings at our manufacturing sites. Chaired by our site directors and following a set agenda to ensure consistency of approach these meetings form our first line of performance monitoring. Our Health and Safety team work across the sites to ensure learning is not siloed and to prevent similar incidents reoccurring in different facilities. Safety Specialists meet Senior Executives twice per month to review the latest safety data, to consider trends and identify actions needed. In addition, all safety data is reported to the Sustainability Committee on a quarterly basis. Vincenzo Regazzoni, Chief Industrial Officer and member of the Executive Committee, leads on the Company’s safety performance and activities. We have developed a five-year road map including targets designed to drive continual improvement to the safety culture. The annual performance bonus paid to eligible employees has a safety element centred on achieving consistent reductions in safety-related incidents over time.
